Python asyncio — Coroutines and Tasks

This section outlines high-level asyncio APIs for working with coroutines and Tasks.

Coroutines

Coroutines declared with the async/await syntax are the preferred way of writing
asyncio applications. Simply calling a coroutine will not schedule it to be
executed — it returns a coroutine object that must be run through one of several
mechanisms provided by the asyncio module.

To actually run a coroutine, asyncio provides the following mechanisms:

The asyncio.run() function runs the top-level entry point "main()" function and
manages the event loop lifecycle. This is the standard entry point for asyncio
programs.

Awaiting on a coroutine allows you to call other coroutines sequentially within
an async context. The calling coroutine suspends until the awaited coroutine
completes.

The asyncio.create_task() function runs coroutines concurrently as asyncio Tasks.
When a coroutine is wrapped into a Task, it is automatically scheduled to run on
the current event loop.

The asyncio.TaskGroup class, introduced in Python 3.11, provides a modern
alternative to create_task() with stronger safety guarantees. All tasks in a
TaskGroup are cancelled if any task raises an exception.

Awaitables

An awaitable object can be used in an await expression. The three main types of
awaitable objects in asyncio are coroutines, Tasks, and Futures.

Tasks are used to schedule coroutines concurrently. A Task wraps a coroutine
object and manages its execution on the event loop.

Futures are special low-level awaitable objects that represent the eventual result
of an asynchronous operation. When a Future object is awaited, the coroutine will
wait until the Future is resolved in another part of the program. Futures are
sometimes exposed by libraries and asyncio APIs, but generally do not need to be
created at the application level.
